lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent wireless agent businesses and multi-location cellular or mobile-phone stores that repeatedly sell and activate wireless carrier or reseller service plans on a commission basis for unaffiliated carriers or MVNOs, including related plan advice, upgrades, accessories, device setup, and customer assistance when plan agency is the primary activity.
lens.excluded_activities: Carrier-owned stores and captive sales units, telecommunications resellers that buy wholesale capacity, facilities-based carriers, satellite service, stores selling only devices or accessories, general electronics retailers whose primary activity lies elsewhere, independent salespeople without a transferable operating business, shells, and firms without repeat carrier or customer relationships.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The commissioning customer is a wireless carrier or reseller, while households and small businesses purchase plans and devices through the agent. Revenue is typically activation, upgrade, accessory, device, performance, and sometimes residual commissions or incentives governed by carrier agreements, with repeat economics depending on store traffic, conversion, retention, chargebacks, territory, brand authorization, and ongoing carrier relationships.

a.bridge: No public occupation mix isolates 517122. BLS broad NAICS 517000 data are dominated by facilities-based telecommunications, including 29.67% installation and repair, and therefore do not represent commission-agent stores; O*NET retail-sales and retail-supervisor tasks are closer workflow proxies but span all merchandise retail. I map plan and device comparison, promotion and policy retrieval, eligibility and quote preparation, activation data entry, transaction records, inventory, scheduling, coaching support, routine inquiries, and first-pass troubleshooting as exposed, while retaining persuasion, trust, needs discovery, identity and fraud judgment, physical device handling, complex setup, complaint resolution, cash control, staff supervision, and accountable sales conduct as human. The telecom mix likely understates the retail-sales share, while generic retail tasks may understate the technical and regulated complexity of wireless activations.
a.rationale: The code is a commission retail-sales channel. O*NET shows a mixture of structured product, pricing, record, contract, inventory, and policy work suited to assistance, alongside customer-facing influence, demonstrations, payments, security, complaints, and supervision that remain interpersonal and accountable.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task survey of independent wireless-agent stores separating needs discovery, quoting, activation, device setup, accessories, support, inventory, scheduling, cash control, management, and carrier administration would materially change the range.
caveats: The BLS occupation mix is for all telecommunications and is poorly matched to retail commission agents.
O*NET retail-sales and supervisor occupations do not isolate wireless plan activation or carrier compliance.
The balance between in-store selling, device setup, support, and back-office work varies by carrier program and store format.
The injected compensation ratio is an ancestor-code proxy for all NAICS 517 and is likely depressed by capital-intensive facilities-based carrier revenue.

rho.rationale: Carrier portals, point-of-sale systems, digital identity tools, device diagnostics, inventory systems, and structured plan catalogs make comparison, quoting, activation checks, knowledge retrieval, follow-up, scheduling, training support, and routine service assistance implementable within five years. Constraints include carrier-controlled systems, frequent promotions, identity theft and fraud, credit and payment data, accessibility, device diversity, chargebacks, consumer-protection obligations, store-level change management, and the importance of accurate human handling when service or financing terms are consequential.
rho.change_evidence: Controlled store deployments measuring labor per activation, quote and activation errors, fraud, chargebacks, conversion, accessory attachment, repeat visits, complaints, staff acceptance, carrier audit results, and fully loaded tool and integration cost would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No source measures five-year AI implementation in 517122.
Carrier authorization may limit agents' ability to modify workflows or integrate independent tools.
Automation that shifts the customer to carrier self-service removes channel volume and belongs in d5 or o, not rho.

e.rationale: Repeat plan sales under carrier or MVNO agency agreements fit an outsourced distribution-service model, and multi-store operations can have transferable staff, leases, systems, local reputation, and customer traffic. Eligibility is reduced by carrier-owned stores, single-person agencies, device-led retailers misclassified into the code, thinly capitalized stores, at-will or nonassignable carrier contracts, weak customer ownership, concentration in one carrier, poor compliance, lease or territory issues, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
e.change_evidence: A matched Census, carrier authorized-retailer, commercial-company, lease, tax, and transaction sample classifying agents by ownership, store count, normalized EBITDA, carrier mix, contract term and assignability, chargebacks, customer data rights, and compliance standing would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The NAICS definition establishes commission agency activity but not lower-middle-market scale, independence, or transferability.
The carrier rather than the agent may own the subscriber relationship, brand, pricing, and critical customer data.
The frozen dataset has no defensible n-band value, so the relevant firm stock remains mechanically missing.

s5.rationale: Multi-store agent groups can be sold through equity or asset transactions, particularly when they have durable carrier authorizations, trained teams, leases, compliance records, and local operating density. Qualifying transfers are limited by carrier consent, nonassignable or terminable agreements, founder or operator dependence, lease approvals, brand restrictions, chargeback exposure, buyer concentration, and closures or carrier channel withdrawals that do not produce a transferable business.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year cohort of carrier-authorized retailer ownership changes, contract consents, store portfolio sales, private M&A, failed transactions, closures, and eligible-agent stock would materially change the probability.
caveats: No cited source measures five-year qualifying control transfers among LMM wireless agents.
A store sale without carrier authorization or durable commission rights may not transfer the operating economics.
Carrier-led consolidation, terminations, and franchise reassignments can resemble transfers without being arm's-length control acquisitions.

q.rationale: Within an existing commission schedule, faster activation, fewer errors, better staffing, higher conversion, and more consistent follow-up can preserve implemented benefit. The carrier controls plan prices, promotions, eligibility, commission schedules, chargebacks, brand rules, and often the subscriber relationship, allowing it to capture efficiency at renewal or through revised targets. Local competition, employee incentives, rent, device handling, fraud, and customer expectations further reduce retention.
q.change_evidence: Carrier-contract and store-cohort data showing commission schedules, residuals, incentives, chargebacks, conversion, staffing cost, contract renewals, carrier repricing, audit adjustments, and contribution margin before and after automation would materially change retention.
caveats: No source measures retention of implemented AI benefits in this industry.
Commission and chargeback terms are private and can change unilaterally or at short renewal intervals.
Efficiency may be competed away through longer service, lower accessory pricing, or carrier performance requirements.

d5.bridge: BLS projects constant-dollar output for all NAICS 517000 Telecommunications to grow from $674.3 billion in 2024 to $876.8 billion in 2034, or 2.7% annually, not demand for commission-agent sales and activation. I start from continued wireless connectivity, device replacement, and plan-change demand, then apply a material downward channel adjustment for carrier websites and apps, eSIM, remote support, automated activation, owned stores, and direct fulfillment. Broad telecom output therefore likely overstates agent-channel demand; local in-person needs, device complexity, fraud controls, and underserved customer segments could make the low end too pessimistic.
d5.rationale: Wireless service remains essential, but the agent is a distribution channel rather than the network service itself. Physical agents can retain demand for advice, immediate devices, financing, setup, identity handling, troubleshooting, and customers who prefer or need in-person support, while straightforward upgrades and activations increasingly lend themselves to direct digital channels.
d5.change_evidence: Carrier and agent cohorts showing activations, upgrades, gross adds, store traffic, online and app share, eSIM activations, device financing, support visits, agent-store openings and closures, and constant-price commission revenue would materially change the ratio.
caveats: The BLS projection covers all telecommunications output and does not report distribution channel mix.
No source quantifies five-year agent-channel share, eSIM substitution, or independent-store traffic.
Agent demand varies by prepaid versus postpaid, consumer demographics, carrier strategy, geography, and device complexity.

o.rationale: Carrier apps and websites, eSIM, automated eligibility and activation, direct device shipment, video support, and software diagnostics can eliminate a large share of basic plan comparison, upgrade, activation, and troubleshooting quantity. An accountable local agent remains useful for complex needs discovery, multi-line or business accounts, immediate device fulfillment, trade-ins, financing and identity checks, physical setup, data transfer, fraud-sensitive exceptions, accessibility, complaints, and customers who value in-person service.
o.change_evidence: Direct-versus-agent activation and upgrade data, eSIM adoption, digital completion and abandonment rates, in-store support reasons, device and trade-in attachment, identity-fraud outcomes, carrier channel economics, and agent-store closure cohorts would materially change this share.
caveats: No source directly measures the future operator-required share for wireless commission agents.
The carrier remains the accountable service provider even where an agent performs the customer-facing transaction.
Digital self-service may complement stores for complex cases rather than eliminate them uniformly.

narrative.executive_view: Wireless agents are commission-based distribution businesses with repeat carrier and customer interactions, but they do not own the underlying service, pricing, brand, or usually the subscriber relationship. AI can improve store execution and administration, while carrier-controlled digital channels create direct structural substitution risk.
narrative.ai_impact: Useful applications include plan and promotion retrieval, needs-assessment prompts, quotes, activation checks, knowledge support, device diagnostics, follow-up, scheduling, training, inventory planning, complaint classification, and transaction review. Humans remain important for persuasion, trust, identity and fraud judgment, device handling and setup, trade-ins, complex accounts, complaints, cash control, and accountable supervision.
narrative.value_capture: Agents can retain savings while commission terms remain fixed, but carriers can revise payouts, targets, chargebacks, territories, and channel strategies. The agent's weak control over price, product, customer data, and contract renewal constrains durable value capture.
narrative.firm_availability: Independent multi-store groups can have repeat outsourced distribution economics and transferable local operations. Carrier consent, terminable agreements, customer ownership, concentration, leases, compliance, and the missing n-band input prevent a defensible estimate of the available LMM stock.
narrative.demand_durability: Wireless connectivity and device replacement are durable, but independent agents sell a channel service that can migrate to carrier apps, websites, eSIM, owned stores, and direct fulfillment. In-person advice, setup, immediate inventory, identity handling, and complex exceptions preserve a reduced role.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are carrier concentration, contract termination or nonassignment, commission cuts and chargebacks, direct-channel substitution, eSIM and remote activation, store traffic decline, lease obligations, device inventory and shrink, fraud and identity theft, employee turnover, consumer complaints, financing and payment compliance, local competition, brand dependence, and the missing lower-middle-market firm count.
narrative.principal_driver: Structured plan, promotion, activation, training, scheduling, inventory, and transaction workflows create practical assistive-AI opportunities at store level.
narrative.principal_weakness: Carriers control the product, economics, authorization, customer relationship, and competing digital channel, limiting transferability and durable agent demand.

sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual
sources.supports: Defines 517122 as agents for carriers and resellers selling wireless plans on commission, lists cellular and mobile-phone stores and MVNO agents, and distinguishes carriers, resellers, and device-only retailers.

sources.title: BLS May 2023 Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 517000 Telecommunications
sources.supports: Provides the broad telecom occupation mix and demonstrates why sector data require a strong bridge for retail agents, including 29.67% installation and repair, 17.82% sales, and 14.85% office support.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Retail Salespersons
sources.supports: Describes customer needs discovery, recommendations, pricing and payment, product questions and demonstrations, promotion and security knowledge, inventory, records, contracts, returns, and service-contract sales.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: First-Line Supervisors of Retail Sales Workers
sources.supports: Describes customer inquiries and complaints, staff supervision, inventory, cash reconciliation, sales monitoring, difficult transactions, and duty assignment.

sources.title: BLS Employment and Output by Industry, 2024-2034
sources.supports: Projects broad telecommunications constant-dollar output from $674.3 billion in 2024 to $876.8 billion in 2034, a 2.7% annual rate, while employment declines 0.9% annually.
